1:
"And I, brethren, when I came to you, came not with excellency of speech or of wisdom, declaring unto you the testimony of God." The witnesses note Paul's plain preaching, not with excellency of speech. On "came not with excellency of speech or of wisdom," the grammatical voices note the manner. Barnes and Robertson note Paul reminds them how he came: not with superior eloquence (excellency of speech) or philosophical wisdom — deliberately renouncing the rhetorical display the Greeks admired.

On "declaring the testimony of God," the witnesses note the substance. Barnes and Calvin note Paul declared (announced plainly) the testimony of God — the witness God gave concerning Christ (or the gospel itself) — content with the bare message, not adorned with human art.

The eastern Chrysostom and latin Tertullian note the design: Paul's plain manner was deliberate, that the gospel's power, not the preacher's skill, should be seen. The verse displays Paul's plain preaching, not with excellency of speech.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul reminds them he came not with superior eloquence or philosophical wisdom, deliberately renouncing the rhetorical display the Greeks admired (Barnes, Robertson), declaring plainly the testimony of God — the witness concerning Christ — content with the bare message, not adorned with human art (Barnes, Calvin). Chrysostom and Tertullian note Paul's plain manner was deliberate, that the gospel's power, not the preacher's skill, should be seen. The verse displays Paul's plain preaching. The verse marks the manner: not excellency of speech. The verse displays the substance: the testimony of God. The verse displays the design: deliberate plainness. The verse displays the aim: the gospel's power. The plain preaching displays Paul's renunciation of rhetorical display. The verse displays the speech: not excellent. The verse displays the testimony: of God. The verse displays the purpose: power over skill. Paul's plain preaching, not with excellency of speech, displays his deliberate renunciation of rhetorical art: he came declaring the bare testimony of God, refusing the eloquence and philosophy the Greeks prized, that the gospel's power and not the preacher's skill might appear. The verse displays the apostle's chosen simplicity, and names its single theme (v.2).
2:
"For I determined not to know any thing among you, save Jesus Christ, and him crucified." The witnesses note Paul's resolve to know nothing but Christ crucified. On "I determined not to know any thing... save Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the resolve. Barnes and Robertson note Paul made a deliberate determination (resolution) to confine his preaching to one theme — Jesus Christ — setting aside all else as his subject among them.

On "and him crucified," the witnesses note the focus. Barnes and Calvin note the emphasis falls on the crucified Christ — the very point of offence to Jew and Greek (1:23); Paul made the cross, not philosophy, the centre of all his preaching.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the principle: the whole of saving knowledge centres in the crucified Christ; Paul resolved to know and preach nothing else, the cross being the sum of the gospel. The verse displays Paul's resolve to know nothing but Christ crucified.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul made a deliberate determination to confine his preaching to one theme — Jesus Christ — setting aside all else (Barnes, Robertson); the emphasis falling on the crucified Christ, the very point of offence to Jew and Greek, made the centre of all his preaching (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note the whole of saving knowledge centres in the crucified Christ, the cross being the sum of the gospel. The verse displays Paul's resolve. The verse marks the determination: to know nothing else. The verse displays the theme: Jesus Christ. The verse displays the focus: him crucified. The verse displays the principle: the cross the sum. The resolve displays Paul's single-minded focus on the cross. The verse displays the determination: deliberate. The verse displays the subject: Christ alone. The verse displays the centre: the crucified. Paul's resolve to know nothing but Christ crucified displays his single-minded focus: he deliberately confined his preaching to the crucified Christ — the offence to Jew and Greek — making the cross, not human wisdom, the whole sum and centre of his message. The verse displays the apostle's concentration on the cross, and describes the weakness in which he preached it (vv.3-5).
3:
"And I was with you in weakness, and in fear, and in much trembling." The brief verse notes Paul's weakness, fear, and trembling among them. The grammatical voices note the manner. Barnes and Robertson note Paul confesses he was with them in weakness (bodily frailty, or the absence of imposing presence), and in fear and much trembling — a deep sense of his own insufficiency and the weight of his task.

On "fear and much trembling," the witnesses note the disposition. Barnes notes this was not cowardice but a holy anxiety — a humble, earnest concern lest he fail in so great a work, and a dependence on God rather than self.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: Paul's evident weakness made the power of his preaching manifestly God's, not his own — the treasure shining the brighter in an earthen vessel. The verse displays Paul's weakness, fear, and trembling among them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul confesses he was with them in weakness (bodily frailty, or the absence of imposing presence) and in fear and much trembling — a deep sense of his insufficiency and the weight of his task (Barnes, Robertson); not cowardice but a holy anxiety, a humble concern lest he fail, and dependence on God rather than self (Barnes). Calvin notes Paul's evident weakness made the power of his preaching manifestly God's, the treasure shining brighter in an earthen vessel. The verse displays Paul's weakness. The verse marks the frailty: weakness. The verse displays the disposition: fear and trembling. The verse displays the cause: a sense of the task. The verse displays the effect: power seen as God's. The weakness displays Paul's humble dependence on God. The verse displays the frailty: bodily. The verse displays the anxiety: holy. The verse displays the dependence: on God. Paul's weakness, fear, and trembling among them display his humble dependence on God: he came not in self-confident strength but in conscious frailty and earnest anxiety, that the power of his preaching might be seen to be God's and not his own. The verse displays the apostle's weakness as the foil to the Spirit's power, which he describes next (vv.4-5).
4:
"And my speech and my preaching was not with enticing words of man's wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power:" The witnesses note Paul's preaching in demonstration of the Spirit and power, not enticing words. On "not with enticing words of man's wisdom," the grammatical voices note the renunciation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's speech and preaching were not with "enticing (persuasive, alluring) words of man's wisdom" — not the artful rhetoric and plausible arguments of the philosophers.

On "in demonstration of the Spirit and of power," the witnesses note the true power. Barnes and Calvin note instead his preaching came "in demonstration of the Spirit and of power" — the convincing, converting work of the Holy Spirit, and the power (whether of miracles or of the Spirit's inward energy) that attended the word.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the contrast: human eloquence persuades by art; the Spirit demonstrates by power, convincing the heart with a certainty no rhetoric can give. The verse displays Paul's preaching in demonstration of the Spirit and power.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's speech and preaching were not with "enticing words of man's wisdom" — not the artful rhetoric of the philosophers (Barnes, Robertson) — but "in demonstration of the Spirit and of power," the convincing, converting work of the Holy Spirit and the power that attended the word (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril and Calvin note human eloquence persuades by art, but the Spirit demonstrates by power, convincing the heart with a certainty no rhetoric can give. The verse displays Paul's preaching in the Spirit's power. The verse marks the renunciation: not enticing words. The verse displays the power: demonstration of the Spirit. The verse displays the contrast: art vs power. The verse displays the certainty: the Spirit's conviction. The preaching displays the Spirit's power over human eloquence. The verse displays the words: not alluring. The verse displays the demonstration: of the Spirit. The verse displays the power: attending the word. Paul's preaching in demonstration of the Spirit and power, not enticing words, displays the true source of its effect: he renounced the persuasive rhetoric of human wisdom, and his message came with the convincing, converting power of the Holy Spirit, which alone gives the heart a certainty no eloquence can produce. The verse displays the divine power as the ground of gospel conviction, and names its purpose (v.5).
5:
"That your faith should not stand in the wisdom of men, but in the power of God." The witnesses note the purpose: faith resting on God's power, not man's wisdom. On "that your faith should not stand in the wisdom of men," the grammatical voices note the design. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's deliberate purpose in preaching plainly: that their faith should not rest upon (be founded on) human wisdom or eloquence — for a faith built on rhetoric would be unstable.

On "but in the power of God," the witnesses note the true foundation. Barnes and Calvin note instead their faith was to rest on the power of God — the divine power they had experienced in the Spirit's work; a faith so founded is sure and abiding.

The Reformed Calvin notes the wisdom of God's method: had faith rested on eloquence, it would stand or fall with the preacher's skill; resting on God's power, it is immovable. The verse displays faith resting on God's power, not man's wisdom.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's deliberate purpose in preaching plainly was that their faith should not rest on human wisdom or eloquence — a faith built on rhetoric being unstable (Barnes, Robertson) — but on the power of God, the divine power they had experienced in the Spirit's work, a faith so founded being sure and abiding (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes had faith rested on eloquence it would stand or fall with the preacher's skill; resting on God's power, it is immovable. The verse displays faith resting on God's power. The verse marks the design: not in man's wisdom. The verse displays the foundation: the power of God. The verse displays the stability: sure and abiding. The verse displays the wisdom: of God's method. The purpose displays faith founded on God, not human skill. The verse displays the avoidance: human wisdom. The verse displays the ground: God's power. The verse displays the security: immovable faith. The purpose that faith should rest on God's power, not man's wisdom, displays the wisdom of Paul's plain method: a faith built on human eloquence would stand or fall with the preacher's skill, but a faith founded on the experienced power of God is sure and immovable. The verse displays the divine power as the firm ground of saving faith, and turns to the true wisdom Paul does impart (vv.6-9).
6:
"Howbeit we speak wisdom among them that are perfect: yet not the wisdom of this world, nor of the princes of this world, that come to nought:" The witnesses note the true wisdom spoken among the mature. On "we speak wisdom among them that are perfect," the grammatical voices note the qualification. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having disparaged human wisdom, now affirms that the gospel is true wisdom — spoken among "them that are perfect" (the mature, the established believers, those able to receive it), not the spiritually immature.

On "not the wisdom of this world, nor of the princes of this world," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes notes this wisdom is not that of the world or its rulers (the great, the philosophers) — a wisdom that "comes to nought" (is passing, perishing, doomed to vanish).

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the point: the gospel is the highest wisdom, but only the spiritually mature discern it; the world's wisdom, however esteemed, is transient and empty. The verse displays the true wisdom spoken among the mature.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, having disparaged human wisdom, affirms the gospel is true wisdom — spoken among "them that are perfect" (the mature, established believers able to receive it), not the immature (Barnes, Robertson); not the wisdom of the world or its rulers (the great, the philosophers), a wisdom that "comes to nought," passing and perishing (Barnes). Chrysostom and Calvin note the gospel is the highest wisdom, but only the spiritually mature discern it; the world's wisdom is transient and empty. The verse displays the true wisdom among the mature. The verse marks the wisdom: spoken among the perfect. The verse displays the contrast: not the world's wisdom. The verse displays the transience: comes to nought. The verse displays the discernment: by the mature. The true wisdom displays the gospel as the highest wisdom for the mature. The verse displays the wisdom: of the gospel. The verse displays the maturity: of the hearers. The verse displays the world's wisdom: perishing. The true wisdom spoken among the mature displays the gospel as the highest wisdom, discerned by the spiritually established: not the wisdom of this world or its doomed rulers, but a divine wisdom that the mature alone can receive. The verse displays the gospel as true wisdom over against the world's, and unfolds its hidden, eternal character (v.7).
7:
"But we speak the wisdom of God in a mystery, even the hidden wisdom, which God ordained before the world unto our glory:" The witnesses note the hidden wisdom of God ordained unto our glory. On "the wisdom of God in a mystery," the grammatical voices note the character. Barnes and Robertson note Paul speaks "the wisdom of God in a mystery" — a wisdom that is God's own, once hidden (a mystery, secret) but now revealed in the gospel.

On "the hidden wisdom, which God ordained before the world," the witnesses note the eternity. Barnes and Calvin note this wisdom was hidden (concealed in former ages) and ordained (fore-appointed, decreed) by God before the world began — an eternal plan, not a later device.

On "unto our glory," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the end: God ordained this hidden wisdom for our glory — the salvation and glorification of believers being its appointed purpose. The verse displays the hidden wisdom of God ordained unto our glory.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul speaks "the wisdom of God in a mystery" — a wisdom that is God's own, once hidden but now revealed in the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); hidden in former ages and ordained (fore-appointed) by God before the world began, an eternal plan not a later device (Barnes, Calvin); and ordained "unto our glory," the salvation and glorification of believers being its purpose (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays the hidden wisdom of God. The verse marks the character: wisdom in a mystery. The verse displays the eternity: ordained before the world. The verse displays the end: unto our glory. The verse displays the revelation: now made known. The hidden wisdom displays God's eternal plan for our glory. The verse displays the mystery: once hidden. The verse displays the ordaining: before the world. The verse displays the glory: of believers. The hidden wisdom of God ordained unto our glory displays the eternal, gracious plan embodied in the gospel: a wisdom that is God's own, concealed in former ages but decreed before the world began for the glory of his people, now revealed in the cross. The verse displays the eternal depth of the gospel wisdom, and shows why the world's rulers missed it (v.8).
8:
"Which none of the princes of this world knew: for had they known it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory." The witnesses note the rulers' ignorance and the crucifixion of the Lord of glory. On "none of the princes of this world knew," the grammatical voices note the ignorance — and a debate. Most (Barnes, Clarke, Gill, Calvin) take the "princes of this world" as the human rulers who crucified Christ — both the Jewish leaders (Scribes, Pharisees, priests) and the Roman authorities (Pilate); Gill explicitly rejects the view that demonic powers are meant.

On "had they known it, they would not have crucified," the witnesses note the proof. Barnes and Robertson note their ignorance is proved by the crucifixion: had the rulers understood God's hidden wisdom and Christ's true dignity, they would not have crucified him (Acts 3:17).

On "the Lord of glory," the witnesses note the deity of Christ. Wesley, Barnes, and Robertson note this august title — applied to Jehovah in the Old Testament (Psalm 24) — is here given to the crucified Christ, plainly declaring his deity; the Lord of glory was crucified. The verse displays the rulers' ignorance and the crucifixion of the Lord of glory.

The witnesses agree the rulers' ignorance is proved by the crucifixion — had they understood God's wisdom and Christ's dignity, they would not have crucified him (Acts 3:17, Barnes, Robertson) — and that "the Lord of glory," a title of Jehovah, is here given to the crucified Christ, declaring his deity (Wesley, Barnes). But "the princes of this world" divides: most take them as the human rulers, Jewish and Roman, who crucified Christ (Barnes, Clarke, Gill, Calvin); Gill explicitly rejects, and some have held, the demonic-powers reading. The verse displays the rulers' ignorance. The verse marks the ignorance: the princes knew not. The verse displays the proof: they crucified him. The verse displays the deity: the Lord of glory. The verse displays the debate: human rulers or demonic powers. The ignorance displays the blindness of the world's rulers to God's wisdom. The verse displays the not-knowing: of the princes. The verse displays the crucifying: of the Lord. The verse displays the glory: his divine title. The rulers' ignorance and the crucifixion of the Lord of glory display the blindness of the world's great to God's hidden wisdom: had they known, they would not have crucified Christ — and that the crucified one is called "the Lord of glory," a divine title, declares his deity. The verse displays the world's blindness and Christ's majesty, with the debate whether the "princes" are the human rulers (the majority) or demonic powers, and grounds the believers' knowledge in revelation (vv.9-10).
9:
"But as it is written, Eye hath not seen, nor ear heard, neither have entered into the heart of man, the things which God hath prepared for them that love him." The witnesses note the things God has prepared — and the debate over their reference. On the citation, the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes (freely, the sense of) Isaiah 64:4 (with phrases from 65:17; 52:15): things unseen, unheard, unimagined by man — beyond all natural discovery.

On the reference, the great debate divides. The popular application takes these words of future heavenly glory. But the grammatical-historical Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, and the Reformed Calvin and Gill argue strongly that the primary reference is NOT future heaven but the present gospel blessings — the wisdom and grace now revealed by the Spirit; for v.10 says "but God hath revealed them unto us," which proves they are already disclosed, not merely awaited.

The witnesses note the indirect application: though the primary sense is the present revelation, the principle may by extension point to the surpassing glory yet to come. The verse displays the things God has prepared, beyond natural discovery.

The witnesses agree the citation (Isaiah 64:4, freely combined) declares things unseen, unheard, unimagined by man — beyond all natural discovery (Barnes, Robertson) — and that they are "prepared for them that love him." But the reference divides: the popular reading applies the words to future heavenly glory, while Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Calvin, and Gill argue strongly the primary reference is the present gospel blessings revealed now by the Spirit, since v.10 ("but God hath revealed them unto us") proves they are already disclosed. By extension the principle may point to future glory too. The verse displays the things God has prepared. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah. The verse displays the things: unseen, unheard. The verse displays the debate: present gospel or future glory. The verse displays the recipients: them that love him. The things prepared display the revelation beyond natural discovery. The verse displays the unseen: by eye. The verse displays the unheard: by ear. The verse displays the unimagined: by heart. The things God has prepared for them that love him display what natural reason can never discover: things unseen, unheard, unimagined — read by most commentators not (primarily) of future heaven but of the present gospel wisdom and grace now revealed by the Spirit (as v.10 shows), though by extension pointing also to the glory to come. The verse displays the gospel's surpassing riches, and shows how they are made known (v.10).
10:
"But God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit: for the Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the deep things of God." The witnesses note the Spirit revealing and searching the deep things of God. On "God hath revealed them unto us by his Spirit," the grammatical voices note the revelation. Barnes and Robertson note that what natural reason cannot discover (v.9), God has revealed to us — first to the apostles, then to all believers — by his Spirit; the gospel's hidden wisdom made known by the Spirit's disclosure.

On "the Spirit searcheth all things, the deep things of God," the witnesses note the Spirit's knowledge. Barnes, Henry, and Cyril note the Spirit searches (knows thoroughly, fathoms) all things, even the deep things (the profound counsels and nature) of God — a knowledge so complete it proves the Spirit's omniscience and deity.

The puritan Henry and eastern Cyril note the doctrine: that the Spirit searches the deep things of God demonstrates his Godhead and distinct personhood — none but God can know God thus. The verse displays the Spirit revealing and searching the deep things of God.

The witnesses agree: what natural reason cannot discover (v.9), God has revealed to us — first to the apostles, then to believers — by his Spirit, the gospel's hidden wisdom made known by the Spirit's disclosure (Barnes, Robertson); and the Spirit searches (knows thoroughly, fathoms) all things, even the deep things of God, a knowledge so complete it proves his omniscience (Barnes, Henry, Cyril). Henry and Cyril note this demonstrates the Spirit's Godhead and distinct personhood — none but God can know God thus. The verse displays the Spirit revealing and searching. The verse marks the revelation: by his Spirit. The verse displays the searching: the deep things of God. The verse displays the omniscience: of the Spirit. The verse displays the deity: the Spirit's Godhead. The Spirit displays the divine source of gospel knowledge. The verse displays the revealing: to us. The verse displays the searching: all things. The verse displays the deity: proved. The Spirit revealing and searching the deep things of God displays the divine source of all gospel knowledge: what reason cannot reach, the Spirit discloses to believers, and his fathoming of the deep things of God proves his omniscience and deity — none but God knows God thus. The verse displays the Holy Spirit as the revealer of the hidden wisdom, and explains how only the Spirit can know God (v.11).
11:
"For what man knoweth the things of a man, save the spirit of man which is in him? even so the things of God knoweth no man, but the Spirit of God." The witnesses note the analogy: only the Spirit of God knows the things of God. On "what man knoweth the things of a man, save the spirit of man," the grammatical voices note the analogy. Barnes and Robertson note the comparison: a man's own inmost thoughts are known only to his own spirit (his self-consciousness) — no other can fully know them.

On "even so the things of God knoweth no man, but the Spirit of God," the witnesses note the application. Barnes and Calvin note the parallel: just so, the deep things of God are known only to the Spirit of God — the Spirit, being God, alone has perfect knowledge of the divine mind.

The eastern Cyril and puritan note the doctrine: the analogy implies the Spirit's deity and his intimate relation to God, as a man's spirit to himself; only one who is God can know God's depths. The verse displays the analogy that only the Spirit of God knows the things of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the comparison — a man's own inmost thoughts are known only to his own spirit (his self-consciousness), no other fully knowing them (Barnes, Robertson) — applied to God: just so, the deep things of God are known only to the Spirit of God, who, being God, alone has perfect knowledge of the divine mind (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril notes the analogy implies the Spirit's deity and intimate relation to God; only one who is God can know God's depths. The verse displays the analogy. The verse marks the comparison: the spirit of man knows the man. The verse displays the application: the Spirit knows God. The verse displays the doctrine: the Spirit's deity. The verse displays the knowledge: of the divine mind. The analogy displays the Spirit's perfect knowledge of God. The verse displays the spirit of man: self-knowledge. The verse displays the Spirit of God: divine knowledge. The verse displays the deity: implied. The analogy that only the Spirit of God knows the things of God displays the Spirit's perfect and divine knowledge of the divine mind: as a man's own spirit alone knows his inmost thoughts, so the Spirit of God alone fathoms the depths of God — a knowledge implying his very deity. The verse displays the Spirit's unique fitness to reveal God, and shows that believers have received this Spirit (v.12).
12:
"Now we have received, not the spirit of the world, but the spirit which is of God; that we might know the things that are freely given to us of God." The witnesses note believers receiving the Spirit to know God's free gifts. On "not the spirit of the world, but the spirit which is of God," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note believers have received not the spirit of the world (the temper and wisdom of the unregenerate world) but the Spirit which is of God — the Holy Spirit himself, given to them.

On "that we might know the things freely given to us of God," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the end of this gift: that we might know (understand, appreciate) the things freely (graciously, as a free gift) bestowed on us by God — the blessings of salvation, discerned by the Spirit's light.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the assurance: the indwelling Spirit enables believers to recognize and enjoy the grace given them; what the world cannot know, the Spirit makes known to God's people. The verse displays believers receiving the Spirit to know God's free gifts.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: believers have received not the spirit of the world (the temper and wisdom of the unregenerate) but the Spirit which is of God — the Holy Spirit himself (Barnes, Robertson) — that they might know (understand, appreciate) the things freely (graciously) bestowed on them by God, the blessings of salvation discerned by the Spirit's light (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril and Calvin note the indwelling Spirit enables believers to recognize and enjoy the grace given them. The verse displays believers receiving the Spirit. The verse marks the contrast: not the world's spirit. The verse displays the gift: the Spirit of God. The verse displays the purpose: to know God's free gifts. The verse displays the discernment: by the Spirit. The reception displays the Spirit given to know God's grace. The verse displays the spirit: of God, not the world. The verse displays the knowing: God's free gifts. The verse displays the grace: freely given. Believers receiving the Spirit to know God's free gifts display the gracious end of the Spirit's indwelling: not the spirit of the world but the Spirit of God has been given them, that they might recognize and enjoy the blessings of salvation freely bestowed — things the world cannot discern. The verse displays the Spirit's work of making known God's grace, and describes how this knowledge is spoken (v.13).
13:
"Which things also we speak, not in the words which man's wisdom teacheth, but which the Holy Ghost teacheth; comparing spiritual things with spiritual." The witnesses note the Spirit-taught words and the comparing of spiritual things. On "not in the words which man's wisdom teacheth, but which the Holy Ghost teacheth," the grammatical voices note the inspiration. Barnes and Robertson note the apostles speak these revealed things not in words taught by human wisdom (rhetoric, philosophy) but in words the Holy Spirit teaches — the very language, as well as the matter, being Spirit-given.

On "comparing spiritual things with spiritual," the witnesses note the disputed phrase. Barnes and Calvin note the phrase (sunkrinontes) may mean expounding spiritual truths in spiritual words (matching spiritual matter with spiritual language), or interpreting spiritual things to spiritual persons, or comparing one spiritual revelation with another — the sense being that spiritual realities are conveyed in their own fitting spiritual terms.

The eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the principle: divine truth is best, and only rightly, expressed in the words the Spirit supplies, and discerned by the spiritually minded. The verse displays the Spirit-taught words and the comparing of spiritual things.

The witnesses agree: the apostles speak these revealed things not in words taught by human wisdom but in words the Holy Spirit teaches — the very language, as well as the matter, being Spirit-given (Barnes, Robertson); and "comparing spiritual things with spiritual" may mean expounding spiritual truths in spiritual words, or interpreting spiritual things to spiritual persons, the sense being that spiritual realities are conveyed in their own fitting spiritual terms (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril notes divine truth is rightly expressed only in the Spirit's words, discerned by the spiritual. The verse displays the Spirit-taught words. The verse marks the inspiration: words the Spirit teaches. The verse displays the phrase: comparing spiritual with spiritual. The verse displays the principle: spiritual matter, spiritual words. The verse displays the discernment: by the spiritual. The Spirit-taught words display the inspired conveyance of divine truth. The verse displays the words: Spirit-given. The verse displays the matching: spiritual with spiritual. The verse displays the fitness: divine terms. The Spirit-taught words and the comparing of spiritual things display the inspired conveyance of divine truth: the apostles speak not in the words of human wisdom but in those the Holy Spirit supplies, fitting spiritual realities to spiritual language and discerning them spiritually. The verse displays the Spirit's authorship of both the matter and the words of revelation, and contrasts the natural man who cannot receive it (v.14).
14:
"But the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God: for they are foolishness unto him: neither can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned." The witnesses note the natural man's inability to receive spiritual things — the great inability crux. On "the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit," the grammatical voices note the subject. Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott note the "natural man" (psychikos) is the unregenerate man, governed by the soul's natural faculties and senses without the Spirit — opposed to the "spiritual" (pneumatikos) man; he does not receive (rejects, has no relish for) the things of God's Spirit.

On "neither can he know them... spiritually discerned," the great debate divides. The Reformed Calvin and Gill read it as total inability: the natural man not only will not but cannot know spiritual things — faith is not in one's own power but divinely conferred; all mankind by nature are destitute of the Spirit and unable to apprehend. The grammatical-historical Barnes qualifies it: the natural man cannot perceive their beauty while he remains sensual and in love with sin, but this does not prove he lacks the natural faculty; his inability is his strong love of sin. Wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads it: as he has not the will, so neither has he the power, lacking the spiritual senses.

The witnesses note the common ground: spiritual things are "spiritually discerned" — perceived only by the Spirit's enlightening. The verse displays the natural man's inability, with the inability crux.

The witnesses agree the "natural man" (psychikos) is the unregenerate, governed by natural faculties without the Spirit, who does not receive the things of God's Spirit, counting them foolishness, because they are "spiritually discerned" (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). But the inability divides them: Calvin and Gill read total inability — the natural man cannot know spiritual things, faith being divinely conferred, all by nature destitute of the Spirit; Barnes qualifies that he cannot perceive their beauty while he remains in love with sin, yet retains the natural faculty, his inability being his love of sin; Wesley holds that as he lacks the will, so he lacks the power and the spiritual senses. The verse displays the natural man's inability. The verse marks the subject: the natural man. The verse displays the verdict: foolishness to him. The verse displays the debate: total inability or moral unwillingness. The verse displays the means: spiritually discerned. The inability displays the need of the Spirit to discern divine things. The verse displays the rejection: receiveth not. The verse displays the incapacity: cannot know. The verse displays the discernment: spiritual. The natural man's inability to receive spiritual things displays the necessity of the Spirit's enlightening: the unregenerate man counts the things of God foolishness and cannot discern them, for they are spiritually perceived — read by the Reformed as a total inability that only divine grace overcomes, and by others as the moral incapacity of a heart in love with sin yet retaining its natural faculty. The verse displays the gulf between the natural and the spiritual, and describes the spiritual man's discernment (v.15).
15:
"But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet he himself is judged of no man." The witnesses note the spiritual man's discernment. On "he that is spiritual judgeth all things," the grammatical voices note the discernment. Barnes and Robertson note the "spiritual" man (the regenerate, indwelt and taught by the Spirit) "judgeth (discerns, examines, rightly estimates) all things" — he can perceive and weigh spiritual realities that the natural man cannot.

On "he himself is judged of no man," the witnesses note the qualification. Barnes and Calvin note the spiritual man is "judged (rightly understood, discerned) of no man" — that is, of no natural man; the unregenerate cannot rightly estimate or sit in judgment on the spiritual man, lacking the discernment to do so.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: the believer, enlightened by the Spirit, has a discernment the world lacks and cannot itself be truly assessed by those without the Spirit — not that he is above all examination, but beyond the competence of the merely natural. The verse displays the spiritual man's discernment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the "spiritual" man (the regenerate, indwelt and taught by the Spirit) discerns, examines, and rightly estimates all things — perceiving spiritual realities the natural man cannot (Barnes, Robertson); yet he himself is "judged of no man," that is, of no natural man, the unregenerate lacking the discernment to estimate or sit in judgment on him (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the believer has a discernment the world lacks and cannot be truly assessed by those without the Spirit. The verse displays the spiritual man's discernment. The verse marks the discernment: judgeth all things. The verse displays the qualification: judged of no man. The verse displays the contrast: spiritual vs natural. The verse displays the competence: only the spiritual. The discernment displays the spiritual man's Spirit-given insight. The verse displays the judging: of all things. The verse displays the immunity: from the natural man's judgment. The verse displays the insight: by the Spirit. The spiritual man's discernment displays the insight the Spirit gives: the regenerate man rightly perceives and weighs spiritual things that the natural man cannot, and is himself beyond the competent judgment of those who lack the Spirit. The verse displays the superiority of spiritual discernment, and grounds it in the believer's possession of the mind of Christ (v.16).
16:
"For who hath known the mind of the Lord, that he may instruct him? But we have the mind of Christ." The witnesses note the believer having the mind of Christ. On "who hath known the mind of the Lord, that he may instruct him?" the grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Isaiah (40:13): no one has known the mind of the Lord so as to instruct or counsel him — God's mind being beyond all human teaching.

On "but we have the mind of Christ," the witnesses note the contrast and gift. Barnes and Calvin note the striking conclusion: though no natural man can know the Lord's mind, "we" (believers, taught by the Spirit) "have the mind of Christ" — we share, through the Spirit, in Christ's own mind and understanding of divine things.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the privilege: the believer, by the Spirit, is admitted to know what is hidden from the world — possessing the mind of Christ, he understands the things of God. The verse displays the believer having the mind of Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Isaiah 40:13 — no one has known the mind of the Lord so as to instruct him, God's mind being beyond all human teaching (Barnes, Robertson) — yet draws the striking conclusion that "we" (believers, taught by the Spirit) "have the mind of Christ," sharing through the Spirit in Christ's own understanding of divine things (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note the believer is admitted to know what is hidden from the world. The verse displays the believer having the mind of Christ. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah. The verse displays the question: who has known the Lord's mind? The verse displays the gift: we have the mind of Christ. The verse displays the privilege: knowing the things of God. The conclusion displays the believer's share in Christ's mind. The verse displays the unknowable: the Lord's mind to nature. The verse displays the gift: Christ's mind to us. The verse displays the privilege: spiritual understanding. The believer having the mind of Christ displays the crowning privilege of the spiritual man: though no natural man can know or counsel the mind of the Lord, believers, taught by the Spirit, possess the mind of Christ and so understand the things of God hidden from the world. The verse displays the believer's Spirit-given participation in Christ's understanding, and closes the great passage on the wisdom of God and the natural and spiritual man.
